title: Daring Raccoon Scales Skyscraper
story: A brave raccoon captured hearts worldwide in 2018 with an incredible feat. On June 13, the furry daredevil began climbing a towering office building in St. Paul, Minnesota. As it scaled the skyscraper's sheer walls, people watched in awe. The raccoon's journey up 23 stories quickly became an internet sensation. Millions followed its progress online, cheering for the tiny climber's safety. Remember, while raccoons are great climbers, it's best to admire wildlife from a safe distance!

story: A fisherman in Cambodia reeled in a whopper on June 13, 2022! In the mighty Mekong River, he caught the largest freshwater fish ever recorded. This discovery highlights the importance of protecting unique aquatic habitats and the incredible creatures that call them home.

title: Comet-Landing Spacecraft Wakes Up!
story: Imagine a tiny robot waking up on a comet far away in space! That's what happened in 2015 when Philae, a special spacecraft, came back to life. Scientists cheered on June 13 when Philae started talking to them again, sending messages from its icy home millions of miles away.

title: The Man Who Hiccupped for 68 Years
story: Charles Osborne's life took an unexpected turn in 1922 when he got a case of hiccups that just wouldn't quit. What started as a normal bodily function became a lifelong companion. Charles hiccupped his way through nearly seven decades, setting an unusual world record. Amazingly, the hiccups finally stopped when Charles was in his 90s. He enjoyed almost a year of hiccup-free living before passing away on June 13.

title: The Artist Behind Star Wars' Look
story: Imagine a world without the iconic look of Star Wars. That's hard to do, thanks to Ralph McQuarrie. Born in Gary, Indiana on June 13, 1929, McQuarrie became the visionary artist who shaped the visual style of the Star Wars universe. His concept art brought to life characters like Darth Vader and C-3PO, influencing the entire film industry. McQuarrie's talent extended beyond a galaxy far, far away - he also contributed to the design of E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial.
activity_question: What famous movie franchise did Ralph McQuarrie help design?
bonus_fact: McQuarrie's art was so influential that a character in Star Wars Rebels was named after him: Garazeb 'Zeb' Orrelios.

title: Racing into History: The First Car Race
story: Vroom! Émile Levassor zoomed across the finish line, making history on June 13, 1895. He had just won the very first automobile race ever held. The Paris-Bordeaux-Paris race was a long and bumpy journey. Levassor drove for nearly two whole days, covering a distance of about 1,178 kilometers. His victory marked the beginning of a new era in transportation and sports.

title: Antarctica's Icy Alarm
story: Imagine a giant ice cube melting faster and faster. That's what's happening to Antarctica! Scientists sounded the alarm in 2018 when they published a report in Nature journal. They discovered Antarctica was losing ice at a mind-boggling rate. Each year, about 200 billion tonnes of ice vanish. Over 25 years, that adds up to 3 trillion tonnes! On June 13, researchers shared this chilling news, urging us to protect our planet's frozen wonderland.

title: Ancient Scottish Islands Older Than Stonehenge
story: Crannogs, mysterious islands in Scottish lochs, held a secret. On June 13, 2019, archaeologists revealed these fortified settlements were far older than anyone imagined. Radiocarbon dating showed they were built between 3640-3360 BC, making them older than the famous Stonehenge! These artificial islands, once thought to be from the Iron Age, now stand as some of the earliest examples of monumental architecture in Europe.
activity_question: What did archaeologists discover about Scottish crannogs?
activity_choices: They were older than first thought
activity_answer: They were older than first thought
follow_up_question: Can you think of other ancient structures that might be older than we currently believe?

title: Chilly California: A Frosty Summer Surprise
story: Brrr! Imagine needing a winter coat in June! On June 13, 1907, Tamarack, California shivered through an unbelievable 2°F. It's a reminder that Mother Nature can always surprise us, even in sunny California!

title: The Inventor Who Typed Up History
story: Clack, clack, clack! The sound of typing filled the air. But who created the first American typewriter? It was William Austin Burt, born on June 13, 1792, in Petersham, Massachusetts. The steam engine and the compass

story: Zoom! In 1910, pilot Charles Hamilton soared into history. He flew from New York to Philadelphia and back in a single day. This daring journey on June 13 was the first of its kind. Hamilton's quick trip showed how air travel could connect distant cities faster than ever before.
activity_question: What two cities did Charles Hamilton fly between?
follow_up_question: How do you think this flight changed people's ideas about travel?

title: Lighting Up the Night
story: Bright orange lights pierced the darkness in Schenectady, New York. On June 13, 1933, the city became the first in the United States to install sodium vapor lamps. These new streetlights glowed with an eerie orange hue, much different from the usual white lights. They lit up the streets more efficiently, helping people see better at night and making the city safer.
activity_question: What color did the new streetlights glow?
follow_up_question: Why do you think better lighting might make a city safer at night?

title: The Sound Scientist is Born
story: A baby's cry echoed through Richwood, Ohio on June 13, 1868. Little did anyone know, that newborn would grow up to become Wallace Sabine, the father of architectural acoustics. Sabine's work revolutionized how we understand sound in buildings, helping create spaces where every note and word can be heard clearly.
activity_question: What field of study did Wallace Sabine become known for?
follow_up_question: Can you think of a place where good acoustics are really important?

title: Ma Ferguson: Texas's Trailblazing Governor
story: Miriam Ferguson, nicknamed 'Ma', blazed a trail for women in politics. Born in Bell County, Texas in 1875, she became the first elected woman governor in the United States. Ma Ferguson served as Governor of Texas for two terms, from 1925 to 1927 and again from 1933 to 1935. Her journey began on June 13, paving the way for future female leaders.
activity_question: What nickname was Miriam Ferguson known by?
follow_up_question: Can you name another U.S. state that has had a woman governor?
